---
title: "RLEF-DataGen"
subtitle: "Harnessing Expert Feedback to Generate Representative Synthetic Clinical Data for Underserved Populations"
author:
  - name: "Sean Sylvia, PhD"
    affiliation: "DHEPlab, UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health"
date: today
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    css: clean-unc.scss
    filters:
      - reveal-header
    header-logo: images/dhep-logo-2.png
    header-logo-link: https://dhep.unc.edu
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    controls: true
    progress: true
    scroll-view: true
    history: false
    hash-type: number
    multiplex: true
    preview-links: auto
    chalkboard: 
      boardmarker-width: 5
    from: markdown+emoji
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: images/RobotDoctor.png
      data-background-size: cover
      data-background-opacity: "0.2"
---


## The Data Representation Gap {.smaller}

### The Problem

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**EHR Adoption Disparities:**

- HICs: **78%** of physicians use EHRs (US, 2021)
- LMICs: **<15%** adoption rates
- Only **27 of 194** WHO countries have national EHR systems
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**AI Training Data Bias:**

- **>90%** of clinical AI datasets from HICs
- **<3%** representation from African populations
- **74%** of AI studies use data from US/China only
:::

::::

> "Clinical AI models risk perpetuating healthcare inequities precisely where they could have the most impact"

::: {.notes}
- US: 96% hospitals, 78% office physicians have certified EHRs as of 2021
- OECD 2023 report: Only 15 of 27 surveyed countries have unified national EHR systems
- PLOS Digital Health 2022: Analysis of 30,576 clinical AI papers showed severe geographic disparities
:::

---

## Synthetic Data as a Potential Solution

### The Growing Use of Synthetic Data 

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**What is Synthetic Clinical Data?**

- Artificially generated patient records
- Preserves statistical properties
- Protects privacy while enabling research
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Market For Synthetic Data:**

- 2023: **$351M** → 2030: **$2.3B**
- **31.1%** CAGR (2023-2030)
- [**75% of businesses** will generate synthetic customer data by 2026]{.alert}, up from less than 5% in 2023 (Gartner)
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
- Gartner prediction on synthetic data usage
- NIH launched N3C with synthetic COVID data - precedent for healthcare
:::

---

## Key Challenges with Synthetic Data 

- **Validation Paradox**: How to verify accuracy without real data?
- **Rare Event Capture**: May miss critical edge cases & comorbidities
- **Bias Amplification**: Risk of encoding generation assumptions as "truth"
- **May not preserve causal relationships**

> Traditionally need a real dataset to train a model to produce (using Generative Adversarial Networks)


::: {.notes}
- Synthetic data challenges: 
  - Validation requires ground truth we don't have
  - Rare diseases/complications often missed
  - Generator biases become "baked in" to all downstream models
- The inverse care law: Those with greatest health needs have least access to data-driven innovations
:::

---

## Current Approaches & Limitations

- **GANs**: Require substantial training data (doesn't solve our problem!)
- **LLMs**: Can generate from limited examples (but quality?)
  
**The Fundamental Challenge**

> How do we generate representative data when none exists for these populations?

**Hypothesis**: We can use local expert knowledge (clinician, providers of various types) to guide generation and validate quality

::: {.notes}
- Key point: Traditional methods still need seed data from the population
- RLEF addresses validation paradox through expert feedback
:::


## Our Approach: Reinforcement Learning with Expert Feedback

::: {.center}
![](images/RLHF.png){width="500"}
:::

#### Note: {.smaller}
RLHF and RLEF are not exactly the same thing. RLHF is about learning what humans like; RLEF is about learning to do what experts do. RLHF handles subjective alignment, while RLEF handles competence transfer.

---

## How RLEF Addresses Key Challenges

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Traditional Limitations:**

- ❌ Need existing data
- ❌ Miss rare events
- ❌ Can't validate without ground truth
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Potential RLEF Solutions:**

- ✓ Bootstrap from expert knowledge
- ✓ Experts flag missing conditions
- ✓ Continuous validation through feedback
:::

::::

---

## Why Not Train AI Models Directly?

#### 1. **Cost-effectiveness**: One dataset → Many applications
#### 2. **Reusability**: Create once, use repeatedly
#### 3. **Flexibility**: Application-agnostic resource

::: {.notes}
- RLEF allows us to bootstrap from zero data
- Experts can identify when rare conditions are missing
- Validation happens through iterative expert review
- Bias monitoring built into the feedback loop
- More efficient to create good data once than retrain many models
:::

---

## The Economics of Expert Feedback 

### Optimization Framework {.smaller}

$$\text{Maximize: } Q(n,e) = f(n \times I(e) \times E(g,s,a)) - CL(e)$$

$$\text{Subject to: } n \times c(e) \leq B$$

**Where:**

- $Q$ = Quality of synthetic data
- $n$ = Number of expert evaluations
- $I(e)$ = Information value per response
- $E(g,s,a)$ = Engagement multiplier (gamification, skills, analytics)
- $CL(e)$ = Cognitive load cost
- $c(e)$ = Monetary cost per response  
- $B$ = Budget constraint

---

## RLEF as a Socio-Technical System 

### **Socio-Technical Systems:** Social and technical elements are fundamentally intertwined. 

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Healthcare Elements: {.smaller}

🔧 **Technology**  
AI algorithms, devices, platforms

👥 **Practitioners**  
Doctors, nurses, administrators

🏥 **Patients**  
Individuals, families, communities
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### System Context: {.smaller}

📋 **Policies**  
Regulations, protocols, guidelines

💰 **Incentives**  
Financial, professional, social

🔄 **Dynamic Interactions**  
All elements continuously influence each other
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Optimizing technical components alone can lead to system-wide failure**
:::

::: {.notes}
Reference Baxter & Sommerville 2011. Think ecosystem analogy - you can't optimize one species without considering the entire food web. Healthcare is particularly complex because of life-or-death decisions.
:::

---

## Traditional AI Implementation Framework: CRISP-DM {.smaller} 

::: {.center}
![](images/crisp-dm-slide1.svg){width=950}
:::


::: {.notes}
CRISP-DM from Chapman et al. 2000. Excellent for traditional business intelligence but assumes you can build and deploy without changing underlying data generating process. Almost never true in healthcare/social systems.
:::

---

## Extended Socio-Technical Framework 

#### **Missing Elements**

- Feedback loops and performativity
- Multiple stakeholders with conflicting objectives
- Dynamic data distributions
- Behavioral adaptation to AI systems

#### **New Components Needed**

- System dynamics mapping
- Continuous behavioral monitoring  
- Intervention design as core activity
- Stakeholder impact assessment

::: {.notes}
This connects to my forthcoming npj Digital Medicine paper. The green boxes are new components. Pink shows the feedback loop that traditional frameworks miss.
:::

---

## CRISP-DM Extended {.smaller}

::: {.center}
![](images/crisp-dm-slide2.svg){width=900}
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Key Addition:** Feedback Loops. Models change the world they operate in
:::

::: {.notes}
This shows how we need to extend CRISP-DM to account for socio-technical realities. The red feedback loop is critical - deployment fundamentally changes the data generating process.
:::

---

## Integration {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Economics / Behavioral Science 
- Incentives 
- Market mechanisms 
- Behavioral economics
- Game theory applications
- Decision-making under uncertainty

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Specialist Knowledge

- Implementation science
- Clinical
- Biomedical
- Data Engineering and Architecture
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
#### Causal Inference

- Moving beyond correlation
- Identification strategies
- Confounding control
- Treatment effect estimation

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Reference Pearl 2009 for causal inference. This is about building a new type of researcher/practitioner. Not enough to be great at ML or great at economics - need systems thinking across disciplines.
:::

---

## RLEF as a Socio-technical System

### AugMed Platform

![](images/AugMed.png)}

---

## Key Socio-Technical Elements

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Technical Components:**

- AI/ML models
- Data infrastructure  
- Platform interface
- Feedback mechanisms
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Social Components:**

- Expert motivations
- Trust dynamics
- Learning effects
- Community norms
:::

::::

> "Healthcare AI systems exist within complex human and organizational contexts"

::: {.notes}
- Drawing from Sylvia's "Digital Health and the Leapfrog Illusion"
- RLEF inherently involves human-AI collaboration
- Success depends on understanding feedback loops
- Platform design must account for behavioral adaptation
:::

---

## Platform Features as Leverage Points

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Cognitive Burden Factors:**

- Task complexity
- Time requirements
- Mental effort
- Decision fatigue
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Engagement Boosters:**

- 🏆 Status badges
- 📊 Skill measurement
- 📈 Peer comparisons
- 🎯 Adaptive learning
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
- Cognitive load is non-linear - too simple = low info, too complex = dropoff
- Platform features can shift the optimal effort point higher
- Gamification particularly effective for routine tasks
- Skill measurement helps weight responses by expertise
:::

---

## Mechanism Design

### Spectrum of Expert Effort

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Low Effort**

- Binary: "Real or Synthetic?"
- Quick response (~30 sec)
- Need many samples
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Medium Effort**

- Pairwise comparison
- "Which is more realistic?"
- Moderate time (~1 min)
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**High Effort**

- Feature-level feedback
- "What's unrealistic?"
- Detailed review (~3 min)
:::

::::

---

### Example Platform UI - Smartphone Version {.smaller}

![](images/ui-mockup.png){height="520"}}


**Here: Clear binary choice** 

---

## Research Questions {.center}

::: {.fragment}
1. What design will maximize information gain per dollar spent?
:::

::: {.fragment}
2. Can the system be designed so it is feasible and scalable? 
:::

::: {.fragment}
3. Would the system be cost-effective, accounting for costs and downstream benefits at scale?
:::

::: {.notes}
- UI designed based on remote interviews with 10 CFK clinicians
- Card flip interaction tested well - creates engagement
- Binary choice (Real/Synthetic) is our "low effort" starting point
- Can extend to collect more detailed feedback on why choice was made
- Mobile-first critical given limited computer access in Kibera
:::

---

## Study Design: Kibera Pilot 

#### Setting 

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Why Kibera?**

- World's largest urban slum
- ~250,000 residents
- Limited healthcare infrastructure
- High disease burden
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Partner: CFK Africa**

- Community health organization
- 20+ years serving Kibera
- Deep local clinical expertise
- Trusted by community
:::

::::

---

## Research Timeline

- ✅ **Phase 1**: Remote interviews with 10 clinicians
- 🔄 **Phase 2**: UI/UX prototyping & testing
- 📊 **Phase 3**: Systems mapping & agent-based modeling
- 🚀 **Phase 4**: 1-month pilot implementation

::: {.notes}
- ABM allows simulation of different intervention strategies
- Can test gamification impact before deployment
- Model expert fatigue and learning curves
- Predict emergence of collective intelligence
- Kibera's constraints make systems approach essential
:::

---

## Expected Impact & Next Steps

### Beyond Technology: Systems Change

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Technical Innovation:**

- RLEF methodology validated
- Cost-effectiveness proven
- Quality metrics established
- Platform features optimized
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Social Innovation:**

- Expert engagement models
- Trust-building mechanisms
- Community ownership
- Sustainable incentives
:::

::::

---

### Path Forward (if successful)

1. **Q3 2025**: Complete pilot & analyze socio-technical dynamics
2. **Q4 2025**: Publish framework with behavioral insights
3. **Q1 2026**: Find someone to pay for full study **Hardest Part**
4. **2026-2030ish**: Design multi-site study with systems perspective
5. **2030 and beyond**: Scale through partner networks

> "Creating AI that learns from and serves those who need it most"

::: {.notes}
- Impact goes beyond technical metrics
- Building sustainable expert communities is key
- Open source enables adaptation to local contexts
- Success = technical quality + social sustainability
:::

---

## Thank You! {.smaller}

### Questions? {.center}

**Contact:**

- Sean Sylvia: ssylvia@email.unc.edu
- DHEPlab: dheplab.org

**Partners:**

- CFK Africa
- UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health

**Funders:**

- Gillings Gift (Gillings Innovation Labs)
- NIH AIM-AHEAD Consortium (previous funding for AugMed Platform)

::: {.notes}
- Thank collaborators
- Mention funding sources if applicable
- Invite questions and discussion
:::

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<!-- Manual references for key statistics and frameworks -->
::: {.references style="font-size: 1em;"}
**Key Sources:**

- OECD (2023). Progress on implementing and using electronic health record systems.
- Celi et al. (2022). Sources of bias in artificial intelligence that perpetuate healthcare disparities. PLOS Digital Health.
- Fortune Business Insights (2023). Synthetic Data Generation Market Forecast 2030.
- ONC/HealthIT.gov (2021). National Trends in Hospital and Physician Adoption of Electronic Health Records.
- Gartner (2023). Predicts 2024: AI and Machine Learning.
- NIH N3C (2020). National COVID Cohort Collaborative synthetic data initiative.
- Sylvia, S. (2025). Digital Health and the Leapfrog Illusion: Socio-technical Systems in Global Health.
:::
